source_label: Larson — Precalculus with Limits (4th edition) — Section 10.2 Introduction to Conics: Parabolas

question_types.label: Conics and Parabolas Vocabulary
question_types.notes: Fill-in-the-blank vocabulary on conic sections, parabolas, focus, directrix, vertex, axis, focal chord, and tangent lines.

questions.notes: general conics vocab, no specific subtopic
questions.unit_guess: Topic PC.GGPE: Expressing Geometric Properties with Equations

ordered_content.value: A ________ is the intersection of a plane and a double-napped cone.

questions.unit_guess: Topic PC.GGPE: Expressing Geometric Properties with Equations

ordered_content.value: When a plane passes through the vertex of a double-napped cone, the intersection is a ________ ________.

questions.notes: vocabulary 'locus'; only loosely tied to GGPE.2
questions.unit_guess: Topic PC.GGPE: Expressing Geometric Properties with Equations

ordered_content.value: A ________ of points is a collection of points satisfying a given geometric property.

questions.unit_guess: Topic PC.GGPE: Expressing Geometric Properties with Equations

ordered_content.value: A ________ is the set of all points (x, y) in a plane that are equidistant from a fixed line, called the ________, and a fixed point, called the ________, not on the line.

questions.unit_guess: Topic PC.GGPE: Expressing Geometric Properties with Equations

ordered_content.value: The line that passes through the focus and the vertex of a parabola is the ________ of the parabola.

questions.unit_guess: Topic PC.GGPE: Expressing Geometric Properties with Equations

ordered_content.value: The ________ of a parabola is the midpoint between the focus and the directrix.

questions.unit_guess: Topic PC.GGPE: Expressing Geometric Properties with Equations

ordered_content.value: A line segment that passes through the focus of a parabola and has endpoints on the parabola is a ________ ________.

questions.unit_guess: Topic PC.GGPE: Expressing Geometric Properties with Equations

ordered_content.value: A line is ________ to a parabola at a point on the parabola when the line intersects, but does not cross, the parabola at the point.

question_types.label: Matching Parabola Equations to Their Graphs
question_types.notes: Visually match a parabola equation in standard form (vertex at origin) with one of four labeled graphs.

stem_header_content.caption: Graphs (a)–(d) for Exercises 9–12
stem_header_content.alt: Four parabola graphs labeled (a),(b),(c),(d): (a) opens up, vertex at origin; (b) opens down, vertex near origin; (c) opens left, vertex on y-axis; (d) opens right (or similar), vertex near origin.

stem_header_content.value: Matching In Exercises 9–12, match the equation with its graph. [The graphs are labeled (a), (b), (c), and (d).]

question_types.notes: Given vertex, focus, and/or directrix not at the origin, write the standard equation (x − h)² = 4p(y − k) or (y − k)² = 4p(x − h).

questions.unit_guess: Topic PC.GGPE: Expressing Geometric Properties with Equations

ordered_content.value:
Slope of a Tangent Line Let $(x_1, y_1)$ be the coordinates of a point on the parabola $x^2 = 4py$. The equation of the line tangent to the parabola at the point is
$$y - y_1 = \frac{x_1}{2p}(x - x_1).$$
What is the slope of the tangent line?

question_types.label: Modeling Real-World Objects with Parabolas
question_types.notes: Application problems in which a parabola models a physical object: reflectors, satellite dishes, road surfaces, beam deflection, fluid streams, archways, suspension bridges, and orbits.

questions.unit_guess: Topic PC.GGPE: Expressing Geometric Properties with Equations

ordered_content.value: Flashlight The light bulb in a flashlight is at the focus of the parabolic reflector, 1.5 centimeters from the vertex of the reflector (see figure). Write an equation for a cross section of the flashlight's reflector with its focus on the positive x-axis and its vertex at the origin.

ordered_content.caption: Figure for 61
ordered_content.alt: Cross-section of flashlight reflector: parabola opening to the right with vertex at origin and focus 1.5 cm from vertex on positive x-axis.

questions.unit_guess: Topic PC.GGPE: Expressing Geometric Properties with Equations

ordered_content.value: Satellite Dish The receiver of a parabolic satellite dish is at the focus of the parabola (see figure). Write an equation for a cross section of the satellite dish.

ordered_content.caption: Figure for 62
ordered_content.alt: Cross-section of satellite dish: parabola opening upward with the receiver labeled and a vertical span of 3.5 ft indicated.

questions.unit_guess: Topic PC.GGPE: Expressing Geometric Properties with Equations

ordered_content.value: Highway Design Highway engineers use a parabolic curve to design an entrance ramp from a straight street to an interstate highway (see figure). Write an equation of the parabola.

ordered_content.caption: Figure for Exercise 63
ordered_content.alt: Parabola opening to the left with points (1000, 800) on the 'Interstate' end and (1000, -800) on the 'Street' end.

questions.unit_guess: Topic PC.GGPE: Expressing Geometric Properties with Equations

ordered_content.value: Road Design Roads are often designed with parabolic surfaces to allow rain to drain off. A particular road is 32 feet wide and 0.4 foot higher in the center than it is on the sides (see figure).

ordered_content.caption: Figure for Exercise 64
ordered_content.alt: Cross-section of a crowned road, 32 ft wide, 0.4 ft higher at the center than at the edges. 'Not drawn to scale.'

ordered_content.value:
(a) Write an equation of the parabola with its vertex at the origin that models the road surface.
(b) How far from the center of the road is the road surface 0.1 foot lower than the center?

questions.unit_guess: Topic PC.GGPE: Expressing Geometric Properties with Equations

ordered_content.value: Beam Deflection A simply supported beam is 64 feet long and has a load at the center (see figure). The deflection of the beam at its center is 1 inch. The shape of the deflected beam is parabolic.

ordered_content.caption: Figure for Exercise 65
ordered_content.alt: A wooden beam 64 ft long, simply supported, with a load at the center causing 1-inch deflection at the midpoint.

ordered_content.value:
(a) Write an equation of the parabola with its vertex at the origin that models the shape of the beam.
(b) How far from the center of the beam is the deflection $\tfrac{1}{2}$ inch?

questions.unit_guess: Topic PC.GGPE: Expressing Geometric Properties with Equations

ordered_content.value: Beam Deflection Repeat Exercise 65 when the length of the beam is 36 feet and the deflection at its center is 2 inches.

questions.unit_guess: Topic PC.GGPE: Expressing Geometric Properties with Equations

ordered_content.value: Fluid Flow Water is flowing from a horizontal pipe 48 feet above the ground. The falling stream of water has the shape of a parabola whose vertex (0, 48) is at the end of the pipe (see figure). The stream of water strikes the ocean at the point $(10\sqrt{3}, 0)$. Write an equation for the path of the water.

ordered_content.caption: Figure for 67
ordered_content.alt: Parabolic stream of water from a pipe at height 48 ft, curving down to ocean level. Vertical scale 0–40.

questions.unit_guess: Topic PC.GGPE: Expressing Geometric Properties with Equations

ordered_content.value: Window Design A church window is bounded above by a parabola (see figure). Write an equation of the parabola.

ordered_content.caption: Figure for 68
ordered_content.alt: Stained-glass church window: rectangular base 8 ft wide topped by a parabolic arch 4 ft tall.

questions.unit_guess: Topic PC.GGPE: Expressing Geometric Properties with Equations

ordered_content.value: Archway A parabolic archway is 12 meters high at the vertex. At a height of 10 meters, the width of the archway is 8 meters (see figure). How wide is the archway at ground level?

ordered_content.caption: Figure for 69
ordered_content.alt: Parabolic brick archway with vertex (0, 12) and points (−4, 10), (4, 10) labeled on the curve.

questions.unit_guess: Topic PC.GGPE: Expressing Geometric Properties with Equations

ordered_content.value: Lattice Arch A parabolic lattice arch is 8 feet high at the vertex. At a height of 4 feet, the width of the lattice arch is 4 feet (see figure). How wide is the lattice arch at ground level?

ordered_content.caption: Figure for 70
ordered_content.alt: Parabolic lattice arch with vertex (0, 8) and points (−2, 4), (2, 4) labeled on the curve.

questions.unit_guess: Topic PC.GGPE: Expressing Geometric Properties with Equations

ordered_content.value:
Suspension Bridge Each cable of a suspension bridge is suspended (in the shape of a parabola) between two towers (see figure). (a) Find the coordinates of the focus.
(b) Write an equation that models the cables.

ordered_content.caption: Figure for Exercise 71
ordered_content.alt: Suspension bridge cable shaped as a parabola: anchored at (−60, 20) and (60, 20), spanning x from -60 to 60 with vertex near origin.

questions.unit_guess: Topic PC.GGPE: Expressing Geometric Properties with Equations

ordered_content.value: Suspension Bridge Each cable of the Golden Gate Bridge is suspended (in the shape of a parabola) between two towers that are 1280 meters apart. The top of each tower is 152 meters above the roadway. The cables touch the roadway at the midpoint between the towers.

ordered_content.caption: Figure for Exercise 72
ordered_content.alt: Photograph of the Golden Gate Bridge showing red towers and suspension cables.

ordered_content.value:
(a) Sketch the bridge on a rectangular coordinate system with the cables touching the roadway at the origin. Label the coordinates of the known points.
(b) Write an equation that models the cables.
(c) Complete the table by finding the height $y$ of the cables over the roadway at a distance of $x$ meters from the point where the cables touch the roadway.

| Distance, $x$ | Height, $y$ |
|---|---|
| 0 | |
| 100 | |
| 250 | |
| 400 | |
| 500 | |

questions.unit_guess: Topic PC.GGPE: Expressing Geometric Properties with Equations

ordered_content.value: Satellite Orbit A satellite in a 100-mile-high circular orbit around Earth has a velocity of approximately 17,500 miles per hour. When this velocity is multiplied by $\sqrt{2}$, the satellite has the minimum velocity necessary to escape Earth's gravity and follow a parabolic path with the center of Earth as the focus (see figure).

ordered_content.caption: Figure for Exercise 73
ordered_content.alt: Diagram of Earth (sphere) with a circular orbit of radius 4100 miles and a parabolic escape path tangent to the orbit. 'Not drawn to scale.'

ordered_content.value:
(a) Find the escape velocity of the satellite.
(b) Write an equation for the parabolic path of the satellite. (Assume that the radius of Earth is 4000 miles.)

question_types.label: Modeling Parabolic Trajectories (Projectile Motion)
question_types.notes: Use a parabolic model x² = −(v²/16)(y − s) to find horizontal range, height, and trajectory of a projectile.

questions.unit_guess: Topic PC.GGPE: Expressing Geometric Properties with Equations

ordered_content.value:
Path of a Softball The path of a softball is modeled by
$$-12.5(y - 7.125) = (x - 6.25)^2$$
where $x$ and $y$ are measured in feet, with $x = 0$ corresponding to the position from which the ball was thrown.
(a) Use a graphing utility to graph the trajectory of the softball.
(b) Use the *trace* feature of the graphing utility to approximate the highest point and the range of the trajectory.

stem_header_content.value:
Projectile Motion In Exercises 75 and 76, consider the path of an object projected horizontally with a velocity of $v$ feet per second at a height of $s$ feet, where the model for the path is
$$x^2 = -\frac{v^2}{16}(y - s).$$
In this model (in which air resistance is disregarded), $y$ is the height (in feet) of the projectile and $x$ is the horizontal distance (in feet) the projectile travels.

children.unit_guess: Topic PC.GGPE: Expressing Geometric Properties with Equations

ordered_content.value:
A ball is thrown from the top of a 100-foot tower with a velocity of 28 feet per second.
(a) Write an equation for the parabolic path.
(b) How far does the ball travel horizontally before it strikes the ground?

children.unit_guess: Topic PC.GGPE: Expressing Geometric Properties with Equations

ordered_content.value: A cargo plane is flying at an altitude of 500 feet and a speed of 255 miles per hour. A supply crate is dropped from the plane. How many *feet* will the crate travel horizontally before it hits the ground?

question_types.label: Conceptual Reasoning About Parabolas and Conics
question_types.notes: True/false reasoning, equivalence of parabola forms, plane–cone intersections that produce each conic, degenerate conics, latus rectum, and area arguments.

stem_header_content.value: True or False? In Exercises 77–79, determine whether the statement is true or false. Justify your answer.

children.unit_guess: Topic PC.GGPE: Expressing Geometric Properties with Equations

ordered_content.value: It is possible for a parabola to intersect its directrix.

children.unit_guess: Topic PC.GGPE: Expressing Geometric Properties with Equations

ordered_content.value: A tangent line to a parabola always intersects the directrix.

children.unit_guess: Topic PC.GGPE: Expressing Geometric Properties with Equations

ordered_content.value: When the vertex and focus of a parabola are on a horizontal line, the directrix of the parabola is vertical.

questions.unit_guess: Topic PC.GGPE: Expressing Geometric Properties with Equations

ordered_content.value:
Think About It Explain what each equation represents, and how equations (a) and (b) are equivalent.
(a) $y = a(x - h)^2 + k,\;\;a \ne 0$
(b) $(x - h)^2 = 4p(y - k),\;\;p \ne 0$
(c) $(y - k)^2 = 4p(x - h),\;\;p \ne 0$

questions.notes: general conic-formation question; broader than just parabolas but lives in section 10.2
questions.unit_guess: Topic PC.GGPE: Expressing Geometric Properties with Equations

ordered_content.value:
HOW DO YOU SEE IT? In parts (a)–(d), describe how a plane could intersect the double-napped cone to form each conic section (see figure).
(a) Circle
(b) Ellipse
(c) Parabola
(d) Hyperbola

ordered_content.caption: Figure for Exercise 82
ordered_content.alt: A double-napped cone shown in three dimensions, used to illustrate conic sections.

questions.unit_guess: Topic PC.GGPE: Expressing Geometric Properties with Equations

ordered_content.value: Think About It The graph of $x^2 + y^2 = 0$ is a degenerate conic. Sketch the graph of this equation and identify the degenerate conic. Describe the intersection of the plane and the double-napped cone for this conic.

questions.unit_guess: Topic PC.GGPE: Expressing Geometric Properties with Equations

ordered_content.value:
Graphical Reasoning Consider the parabola $x^2 = 4py$.
(a) Use a graphing utility to graph the parabola for $p = 1$, $p = 2$, $p = 3$, and $p = 4$. Describe the effect on the graph when $p$ increases.
(b) Find the focus for each parabola in part (a).
(c) For each parabola in part (a), find the length of the latus rectum (see figure). How can the length of the latus rectum be determined directly from the standard form of the equation of the parabola?
(d) How can you use the result of part (c) as a sketching aid when graphing parabolas?

ordered_content.caption: Figure for Exercise 84
ordered_content.alt: Graph of a parabola $x^2 = 4py$ opening upward with the latus rectum drawn through the focus as a horizontal segment connecting two points on the curve.

questions.unit_guess: Topic PC.GGPE: Expressing Geometric Properties with Equations

ordered_content.value:
Geometry The area of the shaded region in the figure is $A = \dfrac{8}{3}p^{1/2}b^{3/2}$.
(a) Find the area when $p = 2$ and $b = 4$.
(b) Give a geometric explanation of why the area approaches 0 as $p$ approaches 0.

ordered_content.caption: Figure for Exercise 85
ordered_content.alt: Parabola $x^2 = 4py$ opening upward with horizontal line $y = b$; the region bounded above by $y = b$ and below by the parabola is shaded.
